summary: Jeff Atwood examines why software often becomes worse rather than better with each upgrade, a phenomenon he calls 'software spoilage.' Using Paint Shop Pro as his primary example, he documents how the application ballooned from 0.4 MB to 326 MB over thirteen years while becoming increasingly cluttered and slow. He argues that in chasing feature bullets and upgrade revenue, companies lose sight of their core values and user needs. The post suggests that lightweight alternatives like Foxit Reader and Paint.NET offer escape from bloated incumbents, while questioning whether any long-lived software can avoid this fate.
key_points: Software spoilage occurs when applications become bloated, slow, and complex through successive upgrades instead of improving
Feature creep is driven by business models that depend on upgrade revenue, creating a misalignment between company incentives and user needs
Paint Shop Pro grew from 0.4 MB to 326 MB over 13 years, exemplifying how software accumulates unwanted features
Lightweight alternatives emerge as responses to bloated incumbents, offering focused functionality without the cruft
The upgrade treadmill eventually makes users 'loathe and fear' the very process that should bring improvements
money_quotes: For every new feature I could actually use and benefit from, there were dozens of other features included that I didn't care about.
Eventually you begin to loathe and fear the upgrade process.
If we inevitably end up making software worse, then why are we bothering?
In trying to be everything to everyone, they progressively destroy that tiny core of uniqueness that they started with.
I just want something that plays my MP3s. I don't need it to burn CDs for me or download new music or cook my breakfast or massage my feet.
Is all software destined to spoil over time?
key_insight: Software spoils when companies prioritize feature accumulation for upgrade revenue over preserving the focused simplicity that made the product valuable in the first place.
